findings.message: Cypher injection: `src`, `dst`, and `kind` are interpolated directly into the AGE Cypher text via f-strings wrapped in single quotes (`{name: '{src}'}`, `[\:{kind}]`). A value containing a single quote (or Cypher metacharacters) breaks out of the literal and lets an attacker inject arbitrary Cypher into the MERGE statement. The docstring's 'pass only trusted, app-controlled values' caveat is not enforced in code, so any caller that forwards user input is exploitable. AGE's cypher() cannot bind parameters, but the values must still be sanitized.
findings.suggestion: Reject/escape single quotes and other Cypher metacharacters in `src`/`dst`/`kind` before interpolation (e.g. validate against an allowlist pattern, or escape `'` by doubling it and disallow backslashes), and constrain `kind` to a known set of relationship types.

findings.message: Cypher injection: `name` is interpolated unsanitized into the MATCH clause (`{name: '{name}'}`) of the AGE cypher() call. A `name` value containing a single quote escapes the string literal and permits arbitrary Cypher injection. Same root cause as `relate()` — the 'trusted values only' docstring is not enforced.
findings.suggestion: Validate/escape `name` before interpolation (escape `'`, reject backslashes/control chars), since AGE cannot bind it as a parameter.

findings.message: The /ws WebSocket endpoint accepts every connection with no authentication, authorization, or Origin check, then broadcasts each received message to all connected clients. This allows cross-site WebSocket hijacking (a browser on any origin can open the socket since WebSocket handshakes are not subject to the same-origin policy) and lets any client broadcast arbitrary messages to all others. Broken access control on a public realtime surface.
findings.suggestion: Authenticate the handshake (token/cookie validation before `accept()`), validate the Origin header against an allowlist, and scope/authorize what a connection may broadcast or receive.

findings.message: `find_documents` passes the caller-supplied `query` Mapping straight into pymongo's `find()`. If any caller forwards request-derived data into this filter, an attacker can inject MongoDB query operators (e.g. `$where`, `$gt`, `$ne`, `$regex`) for NoSQL injection / auth bypass / DoS. The helper provides no operator stripping or shape validation, so the safety depends entirely on every future caller doing it.
findings.suggestion: Validate/whitelist the filter shape (reject keys beginning with `$`) or require typed query parameters rather than an arbitrary Mapping when the filter may originate from untrusted input.

findings.message: The /metrics endpoint is exposed without any authentication or network restriction and returns internal operational data (request counts, error rates, latency, DLQ depth, circuit-breaker state, webhook/ws/graphql counters). If reachable beyond the trusted scrape network this leaks operational details useful for reconnaissance. Acceptable when bound to an internal network, but the route itself enforces nothing.
findings.suggestion: Restrict /metrics to the monitoring network (bind/firewall, mTLS, or a bearer token) rather than relying solely on deployment topology, and document the requirement.

findings.message: The OTLP span exporter is configured with `insecure=True`, sending traces over unencrypted gRPC. Trace spans can contain request paths and other potentially sensitive metadata; on an untrusted network this is exposed in cleartext. Fine for an in-cluster collector but should not be the silent default for production wiring.
findings.suggestion: Make TLS configurable (e.g. an `otel_exporter_insecure` setting defaulting to False outside dev) so production exports traces over an encrypted channel.
